abstract:
The selective capture of carbon dioxide in humid environments remains a significant obstacle in environmental chemistry. In this work, we demonstrate that the internal pores of the metal-organic framework IRMOF-74-III can be precisely engineered via covalent functionalization to overcome this challenge. By introducing primary amine groups to the framework (IRMOF-74-III-CH2NH2), we achieved efficient and selective CO2 capture even at 65% relative humidity. 

Our investigation details the synthesis, structural characterization, and gas adsorption profiles of a series of functionalized derivatives, including -CH3, -NH2, -CH2NHBoc, -CH2NMeBoc, -CH2NH2, and -CH2NHMe. Using 13C cross-polarization magic angle spinning (CP/MAS) NMR spectroscopy, we confirmed that CO2 chemically binds to the -CH2NH2 and -CH2NHMe variants through the formation of carbamic species. Adsorption isotherms and dynamic breakthrough experiments reveal that IRMOF-74-III-CH2NH2 is particularly effective, boasting a CO2 capacity of 3.2 mmol/g at 800 Torr. Most importantly, the material successfully removes CO2 from wet nitrogen streams with a breakthrough time of 610 ± 10 s g⁻¹, maintaining its structural integrity and performance throughout the process.
